paper_title: Morphological Variation in Early and Late South American Populations

methods.description: Classification and grouping of skull samples from various American archaeological sites based on chronology and geographical location to study evolutionary history.

steps.description: Samples were classified into 25 groups according to their early or late chronology and spatial location of provenance.
steps.searchable_summary: classify skull samples into groups based on chronology and location

steps.description: Samples were grouped into geographical regions for studying the evolutionary history of human populations that inhabited each area.

methods.description: Measurement of craniofacial variables and preparation of data for morphometric analysis.

steps.description: 30 craniofacial variables were defined on the basis of functional cranial theory.
steps.searchable_summary: define craniofacial variables based on functional cranial theory

steps.description: Missing observations were estimated by multiple estimations.
steps.searchable_summary: estimate missing data using multiple estimations

steps.description: Original craniofacial measurements were used to calculate ratios or Mosimann shape variables.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Mosimann shape variables from craniofacial measurements

methods.description: Perform PCA to analyze morphological differences between early and late Holocene samples.

steps.description: A PCA was performed, based on a covariance matrix of the ratios.
steps.searchable_summary: perform PCA on covariance matrix of ratios

steps.description: The first PC scores provide graphical displays to study between-group morphological differences.
steps.searchable_summary: visualize morphological differences using first PC scores

steps.description: PCAs for each region were done comparing early and late Holocene archaeological sites.
steps.searchable_summary: perform regional PCA comparing early and late Holocene sites

methods.description: Evaluation of evolutionary rates of craniofacial size and shape under the neutral mutation-drift hypothesis.

steps.description: Lynch's Δ evolutionary rate was calculated separately for craniofacial size and shape variables.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Lynch's Δ evolutionary rate for size and shape

steps.description: The Δ rate test allows for the comparison of evolutionary rates for each region.
steps.searchable_summary: compare evolutionary rates across regions using Δ rate test
